dialogue_line_replacements.text: La verdad que no. Estoy muy agobiada con el trabajo.
dialogue_line_replacements.translation: Honestly, no. I'm very overwhelmed with work. (Spain: agobiada = overwhelmed)

dialogue_line_replacements.text: Todavía estoy mosqueada y un poco triste. No sé qué hacer.
dialogue_line_replacements.translation: I'm still annoyed/bothered and a bit sad. (Spain: mosqueada = irritated/bothered)

cultural_notes_overrides.formality: In Spain, emotions are expressed directly. 'Estar agobiado/a' (to be overwhelmed) is very common for work stress. Spaniards appreciate directness in emotional conversations.
cultural_notes_overrides.gestures: Raising eyebrows and exhaling sharply signals frustration or exasperation in Spain.
cultural_notes_overrides.regional_variations: 'Estar mosqueado/a' (irritated/suspicious) is distinctly Spanish. 'Estar hecho polvo' (to be exhausted/devastated — literally 'turned to dust') is widely used. 'Agobio' (overwhelm) is very Spanish.
